messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant which rephrase the following text in your own words.

As an AI assistant, my purpose is to assist and provide assistance. Here is an attempt to rephrase the given text using my own words:

My function is to offer support and aid as an AI assistant. Here, I will try to rephrase the given text to express the same idea using different wording.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: These have actually been my thoughts for several years, but I haven't done it before because I simply didn't like the thought of plastering my face in my cover art—as if it would seem conceited or narcissistic. But I think the benefits for me outweigh my personal reservations. So you might see my face show up in your podcast app soon! (But please do share your opinion with me!)

These thoughts have been on my mind for quite some time now, although I haven't acted upon them earlier due to my aversion to the idea of prominently featuring my face on the cover art. I worried it might come across as self-centered or self-obsessed. However, I believe the advantages of doing so are more significant to me than my personal hesitations. Therefore, it's possible that you will soon notice my face appearing on your podcast app. (But I would greatly appreciate hearing your opinion on this matter!)

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You can design your own podcast cover art with tools like SoVisual.co or Canva. Or you could try hiring a random designer from 99designs or DesignCrowd. But I think your best choice would be to work with a skilled designer who knows the unique qualities of podcast-branding and even has podcasting experience. And that's why I recommend Mark Des Cotes from PodcastBranding.co.

To create your own podcast cover art, you have the option of using tools such as SoVisual.co or Canva. Alternatively, you can explore the possibility of hiring a designer from platforms like 99designs or DesignCrowd. However, in my opinion, the most advantageous choice would be collaborating with a proficient designer who possesses expertise in podcast branding and even has experience in the podcasting realm. That's why I highly suggest considering Mark Des Cotes from PodcastBranding.co.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: “General promotion” is when you're telling someone to follow your podcast and you're not promoting a specific episode. For example, when you get to mention your podcast on another podcast (if you're not talking about the same thing as one of your episodes), in your digital or print marketing materials (business cards, banners, ads, swag, and such), or when you're making any other kind of general promotion for your show as a whole.

"General promotion" refers to the act of encouraging individuals to follow your podcast without promoting a specific episode. This could include scenarios like mentioning your podcast on another podcast, as long as the topic being discussed is not the same as one of your episodes. It also applies to promoting your podcast as a whole through various digital or print marketing materials, such as business cards, banners, advertisements, merchandise, and more. In essence, general promotion encompasses any form of marketing that highlights and promotes your podcast as a complete entity.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Your podcast needs its own domain! Make sure it's branded to your podcast and points to a page that has multiple follow options: most importantly, Apple Podcasts, Spotify, something popular for Android (Google Podcasts or SubscribeOnAndroid.com), and your RSS feed URL.

It is crucial for your podcast to have its own dedicated domain. Ensure that the domain is aligned with your podcast's brand and directs users to a page that offers multiple follow options. These options should include prominent platforms like Apple Podcasts, Spotify, and a popular choice for Android users such as Google Podcasts or SubscribeOnAndroid.com. Additionally, make sure to include your podcast's RSS feed URL to provide comprehensive subscription choices.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: It would be best for the page to detect the person's type of device and automatically show or hide links appropriately. For example, hiding Apple Podcasts from Android devices (since there's no Apple Podcasts app for Android—at least not yet) but showing Google Podcasts or SubscribeOnAndroid.com. And then doing the converse for Apple devices: showing Apple Podcasts but hiding the Android-only options. This requires some complicated backend code. So the easiest solution I recommend for you is the FollowthePodcast.com page you get when your track your podcast with the My Podcast Reviews service I created for you.

To optimize the user experience, it is ideal for the webpage to detect the type of device being used and display relevant links accordingly. For instance, if someone is accessing the page from an Android device, it would be appropriate to hide the link to Apple Podcasts since there is no dedicated Apple Podcasts app for Android (at least not at the moment). Instead, the page should show options like Google Podcasts or SubscribeOnAndroid.com. Conversely, for Apple devices, it would be advisable to display the Apple Podcasts link while hiding the options exclusive to Android. Implementing this functionality requires complex backend coding. Therefore, the simplest solution I recommend is to utilize the FollowthePodcast.com page provided when you track your podcast using the My Podcast Reviews service that I have created specifically for you.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: If possible, don't actually link to your RSS feed if you display a plain RSS option. Instead, display the feed URL or—even better—make the link automatically copy the URL to their clipboard when they tap or click the link. (Try it on my own FollowthePodcast.com/audacity page!)

If feasible, it is advisable not to directly hyperlink your RSS feed when offering a plain RSS option. Instead, consider displaying the feed URL itself or, for an enhanced user experience, enable the link to automatically copy the URL to the user's clipboard when they click or tap on it. You can test this functionality on my own FollowthePodcast.com/audacity page.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Whether you create this multi-option page yourself or let another service create it for you, this is the single page you share when you need a simple call-to-action: like when you're a guest on another podcast, on your marketing materials (a typed domain or QR Code), in conversations, and such.

Whether you choose to personally create this versatile page with multiple options or rely on another service to develop it for you, it serves as the single point of reference that you can share when you require a straightforward call-to-action. This comes in handy when you are a guest on another podcast, utilizing marketing materials like a typed domain or QR code, engaging in conversations, and similar scenarios where a clear and concise approach is needed.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: When people visit your podcast landing page (like I described in the previous point), they have the margin to consider their options and tap or click on what's best for them. You don't want to overwhelm them with options, which is why it's important to show only the options that work on their devices (like you get with the FollowthePodcast.com feature from My Podcast Reviews).

When visitors arrive at your podcast landing page, as described in the previous point, it is essential to provide them with the space and freedom to contemplate their choices and select what suits them best by tapping or clicking. It is crucial not to inundate them with excessive options, which is why it is important to only display the options that are compatible with their respective devices. This can be achieved, for example, by utilizing the FollowthePodcast.com feature offered by My Podcast Reviews, where device-specific options are shown to prevent overwhelming visitors with an abundance of choices.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: This message is still short enough to fit in a tweet. Just remember that some platforms (like Facebook) might embed a widget or “card” for only the first URL. That's great for single-URL posts, but it could be confusing for multi-URL posts like this. If that happens, you can usually “remove the preview” from the post.

This message remains concise enough to be contained within a tweet. However, it is important to keep in mind that certain platforms, such as Facebook, may display a widget or "card" exclusively for the first URL shared. While this feature is advantageous for single-URL posts, it can potentially cause confusion for posts that contain multiple URLs, like this one. If this occurs, you typically have the option to "remove the preview" from the post, allowing for a cleaner and more streamlined presentation.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Even if someone without an Apple device sees that message, they can still see the benefit your podcast provides and copy the title to search their own podcast app. Or they might simply reply asking for a link for their preferred app.

Even if individuals without an Apple device come across this message, they can still recognize the value that your podcast offers. They have the option to manually copy the title and search for it on their own preferred podcast app. Alternatively, they may choose to respond by requesting a direct link for their preferred podcast app, showcasing their interest in accessing your content through their preferred platform.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Generally, the best link to promote is your episode's webpage with its own embedded episode player. That will work on anyone's device! And if you have high-quality show notes or an article, the visitor can potentially even get the information they want from you more quickly, or be further convinced on the value of your episode.

Generally speaking, the most effective link to promote is the webpage dedicated to your episode, which includes an embedded episode player. This ensures compatibility with any device the visitor is using. Additionally, if you provide high-quality show notes or an article along with the episode, visitors have the opportunity to access the desired information swiftly or be further persuaded about the value your episode offers.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: In most Internet contexts, the length and branding of the URL don't matter anymore because it's usually automatically shortened or hidden. But in spoken contexts, like if you're a guest on another podcast to talk about the same topic you covered in an episode, I recommend having a keyword-based redirect on your own domain.

In the majority of online situations, the length and branding of a URL are inconsequential as they are typically automatically shortened or concealed. However, in verbal contexts such as being a guest on another podcast where you discuss a topic covered in one of your episodes, I highly recommend creating a keyword-based redirect on your own domain. This redirect will provide a more memorable and easily pronounceable link that can be shared during spoken conversations, ensuring that listeners can easily access the relevant content.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For example, if you had me on your podcast to talk about this very subject, and my episode provides additional, relevant value beyond our conversation, then I might say, “I covered this in a lot more detail in my episode about it at TheAudacitytoPodcast.com/bestlink.”

Let's say you invited me as a guest on your podcast to discuss the same subject we are currently discussing. If my episode offers additional valuable insights pertaining to our conversation, I might mention something like, "For a more in-depth exploration of this topic, I extensively covered it in my episode available at TheAudacitytoPodcast.com/bestlink." This way, listeners can easily access the episode and benefit from the extended discussion and relevant information provided.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Within the Podcasting 2.0 project, we're working on a new style of follow links, called “Fast Follow.” This would allow a Podcasting 2.0 podcast app to use your camera to scan a QR Code or even a URL and jump straight to that podcast inside the same app and without leaving the app!

As part of the Podcasting 2.0 project, we are currently developing a fresh approach for follow links known as "Fast Follow." This innovative feature enables a Podcasting 2.0 podcast app to utilize your device's camera to swiftly scan a QR Code or even a URL. Consequently, users can seamlessly and instantly transition to the desired podcast, all within the same app, without the need to exit or switch between different applications.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You might have experienced this same kind of thing at conferences, like Podcast Movement, where people have QR Codes on their badges. If you scan the QR Code with your normal camera app, it opens your browser to a page about that person. But if you scan the same QR Code with the conference app, it either takes you directly to his or her profile inside the app, or it might instantly connect you with each other and share your contact information with each other. Brilliant! And that's the kind of fast simplicity we're bringing to podcast-follow links.

You may have encountered a similar concept at conferences like Podcast Movement, where attendees display QR Codes on their badges. When scanning these QR Codes using your device's default camera app, it typically directs you to a webpage containing information about that individual. However, when using the conference app to scan the same QR Codes, it either leads you directly to their profile within the app or instantly facilitates a connection between you, allowing for the seamless exchange of contact information. This brilliant functionality exemplifies the kind of swift simplicity that we aim to incorporate into podcast follow links.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I understand that this can be daunting. That's why I made this simple for you with the FollowthePodcast.com feature you get included when you track your podcast's social proof through My Podcast Reviews. You get a clean and simple page that smartly shows and hides options depending on your visitor's device. Plus, you can customize the URL so it's easily speakable. For example, I can say, “Follow the podcast to get every episode automatically through FollowthePodcast.com/audacity!”

I can comprehend that this may seem intimidating. In order to simplify this process for you, I have created the FollowthePodcast.com feature, which is included when you track your podcast's social proof using My Podcast Reviews. This feature provides you with a clean and user-friendly page that intelligently displays and conceals options based on the visitor's device. Moreover, you have the flexibility to customize the URL so that it is easily shareable and pronounceable. For instance, you could say, "To automatically receive every episode, follow the podcast through FollowthePodcast.com/audacity!"

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For example, imagine you have a podcast about dogs and you invite me as a guest. Even though I've had a couple of dogs, I'm known for talking about podcasting and helping podcasters. So your audience wouldn't know me. And any “celebrity” or influencer status I might have is constrained to a couple of small niches, and “dogs” is not one of them.

To illustrate, let's envision a scenario where you host a podcast centered around dogs and extend an invitation for me to be a guest. Although I do possess knowledge and expertise in podcasting while providing assistance to podcasters, my familiarity among your audience may be limited. Any recognition or influence I have as a "celebrity" or influencer is confined to a few niche areas, none of which includes the realm of "dogs."

Rephrase the following text in your own words: This usually works better for names of people outside of mass popularity. For example, while Pat Flynn is not a mainstream celebrity, he's been a guest on so many podcasts and there's been so much content about him that you probably wouldn't receive much of an SEO boost by being yet another podcast that interviews Pat Flynn. Thus, there's not as much SEO value in his name alone anymore.

This approach tends to yield more beneficial results when applied to individuals who are not widely recognized in terms of mass popularity. Consider Pat Flynn as an example. Although he is not a mainstream celebrity, he has appeared as a guest on numerous podcasts and there is an abundance of content about him. As a result, featuring him on your podcast as yet another interview might not contribute significantly to your SEO visibility. Consequently, relying solely on his name may not offer substantial SEO value anymore.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: However, if there's someone strongly associated with your own niche and known within it, then their name could provide huge SEO benefit to your podcast. For example, if you interview a cast member from a TV show you podcast about. They're probably a true celebrity, but there's not a flood of content about them and interviews with specifically that one person. So the associations with their name can help boost your SEO.

On the other hand, if you can establish a strong association between an individual and your specific niche, and they are well-known within that niche, featuring their name can potentially bring significant SEO benefits to your podcast. For instance, consider the scenario where you interview a cast member from a television show that aligns with your podcast's theme. Although they may be a renowned celebrity, there likely isn't an overwhelming amount of content or interviews specifically focused on that individual. As a result, the inclusion of their name in your content can contribute to enhancing your podcast's SEO by leveraging their association with your niche.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: If you can focus your conversation around your guest's expertise (and I recommend that you do!), then having their name in your descriptive episode title will help boost their SEO, authority, and influence. This works by associating those special keywords in your title with their name.

By centering your conversation around the expertise of your guest (which I highly recommend), incorporating their name in the descriptive title of the episode can substantially enhance their SEO, authority, and influence. This strategy operates by establishing a connection between their name and relevant keywords included in your episode title. As a result, their online visibility and credibility receive a significant boost, bolstering their overall impact and influence in the industry.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Let's go back to the example of having me on your podcast about dogs. Although I like dogs (and they're much better than cats!), that's not a subject for which I'm trying to build any authority or influence. Thus, I would be less interested in having my name associated with the subject. But if you have me on your podcast to talk about getting more podcast reviews, that is a subject I want to be known for and would appreciate having my name associated with those relevant keywords.

Let's revisit the previous example of having me as a guest on your podcast focused on dogs. While I do have an affinity for dogs (and personally prefer them over cats!), establishing authority or influence in that particular subject matter is not my objective. Therefore, I would be less inclined to have my name associated with the topic.
